refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature database) and can be queried by downstream planning and execution functions to retrieve spatial attributes (position, geometry, classification) for an agreed set of site features.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and restricted areas in the spatial model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and provide access to the worksite spatial model for planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign feature capture responsibility to zone-specific acquisition modules; merge zone models via spatial registration and conflict resolution; expose unified model through a feature query interface.

DP3: Implement a real-time streaming pipeline that ingests raw sensor observations, applies incremental feature extraction and classification, and maintains a versioned spatial database; provide read-only snapshots and change notifications to downstream consumers.

DP4: Establish a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints) and enforce capture/representation conformance through schema validation; decouple feature acquisition from representation by mapping sensor outputs to schema-compliant entities.

DP5: Delegate spatial feature capture to external specialized service (e.g., surveying, photogrammetry, LiDAR provider); ingest delivered model, validate against operational safety/planning requirements, and adapt to internal representation standard.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4 (with DP1 as complementary realization): Establish a declarative feature schema defining geometry primitives, safety attributes, and operational constraints; enforce capture/representation conformance through schema validation. Couple with multi-modal sensing fusion to acquire and map raw observations into schema-compliant spatial entities. This approach decouples the *what* (feature schema, safety/planning requirements) from the *how* (sensor modality, fusion algorithm), enabling independent evolution of acquisition technology and feature semantics.

potential_sources: Schema evolution risk: Changes to safety policy or planning requirements may necessitate schema updates; mitigate via versioning and backward-compatibility rules.
Sensor-to-schema mapping: Tight coupling between specific sensor modalities and feature extraction logic; mitigate by isolating sensor drivers and using adapter pattern for schema mapping.
Real-time vs. batch tension: If planning/execution demand low-latency updates, streaming pipeline (DP3) may be required; ensure schema supports incremental updates and partial models.

External service dependency (DP5 alternative): If outsourced capture is chosen, dependency on third-party data format and delivery schedule; mitigate via adapter layer and acceptance criteria validation.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 + DP1 combination achieves separation of concerns: schema defines the contract (what features, what attributes), sensing/fusion implements the mechanism (how to acquire). This supports independent testing, technology substitution, and policy evolution. Coupling is primarily at the schema-to-sensor-driver boundary, which is manageable via well-defined adapters.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable or change infrequently enough to support schema versioning. Risk: Rapid policy changes may require frequent schema updates and re-validation of existing models. Mitigation: Establish policy change governance and schema evolution process.

Assumption: Multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) is available and can be fused with acceptable latency and accuracy. Risk: Sensor availability, calibration, or environmental conditions (e.g., poor lighting, GPS denial) may degrade model quality. Mitigation: Define sensor requirements and fallback strategies in acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Feature classification and safety attribute assignment can be automated or semi-automated. Risk: Misclassification or incomplete attribute capture may violate safety requirements. Mitigation: Implement human-in-the-loop validation and audit trails.

Assumption: Planning/execution functions can consume a queryable spatial model interface. Risk: Legacy or tightly-coupled planning systems may require custom adapters. Mitigation: Define query interface early and validate compatibility with downstream consumers.

Assumption: Spatial registration and coordinate frame consistency are achievable across acquisition modalities. Risk: Systematic errors or drift in registration may accumulate, degrading model fidelity. Mitigation: Establish registration validation and periodic recalibration procedures.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Required feature types and attributes (e.g., obstacles, hazard zones, access points, equipment locations) derived from safety plan and planning requirements; (2) Acceptable model latency, update frequency, and accuracy tolerances; (3) Query interface contract (e.g., spatial range queries, feature type filters, attribute predicates); (4) Versioning and change notification semantics. This will enable DP4 schema design to be validated against concrete requirements and reduce ambiguity in IM packaging (e.g., whether schema validation is a separate module or embedded in the acquisition pipeline).

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for declarative feature schema definition, schema validation, safety attribute enforcement, and multi-modal sensing fusion orchestration. Owns the canonical representation of geometry primitives, operational constraints, and safety-critical spatial entities. Coordinates sensor data ingestion and conformance mapping without owning individual sensor implementations.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Raw sensor observations (multi-modal: LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) ingested from Vehicle subsystem for fusion and schema mapping

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Schema-validated spatial entities, safety constraint violations, and operational boundary alerts emitted to Vehicle and Teleoperation for decision-making

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operational constraint updates and feature schema refinements from NGHE Solution or Teleoperation to adapt safety/planning requirements

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Optional: distributed schema synchronization or remote safety validation in teleoperation scenarios

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Schema definition, validation, and safety attribute enforcement are core safety responsibilities. Multi-modal fusion orchestration (not individual sensor drivers) naturally belongs here. Decoupling of schema from sensor modality is preserved. Minor diffusion: Vehicle must still own raw sensor hardware drivers; Teleoperation may need read-only access to validated entities for remote monitoring.

Poor fit. Vehicle owns hardware and low-level sensor drivers, but schema definition and safety validation are cross-cutting concerns that should not be embedded in vehicle-specific logic. Placing DP4 here would couple safety semantics to vehicle platform, violating the decoupling principle. Schema would diffuse to Teleoperation and NGHE Solution.

Weak fit. Teleoperation is a consumer of validated spatial entities and safety alerts, not an owner of schema or fusion logic. Placing DP4 here would create tight coupling between remote operation mode and core safety semantics, preventing use in autonomous or other operational modes. Schema would diffuse to Vehicle and Safety Services.

Weak fit. NGHE Solution is a higher-level orchestrator/planner. While it may define task-level constraints, the declarative feature schema and real-time fusion validation are lower-level safety concerns that should not be embedded in solution logic. Placing DP4 here would create inversion of responsibility and tight coupling to planning algorithms.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of DP4 because: (1) schema validation and safety attribute enforcement are intrinsically safety-critical functions; (2) multi-modal fusion orchestration (coordinating heterogeneous sensor inputs into a unified spatial model) is a cross-cutting concern that must remain independent of any single vehicle platform or operational mode; (3) the decoupling of schema (what) from sensor modality (how) is preserved by keeping fusion logic in Safety Services while delegating raw sensor acquisition to Vehicle; (4) Teleoperation, Vehicle, and NGHE Solution can all consume validated spatial entities via SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces without owning the schema or fusion logic.
containment_risks: Risk: Safety Services may become a bottleneck if fusion latency is not carefully managed. Mitigation: design fusion pipeline with explicit latency budgets and consider edge processing for time-critical modalities.
Risk: Schema evolution (Tasking interface) must be carefully versioned to avoid breaking downstream consumers. Mitigation: implement schema versioning and deprecation policies.

Risk: Multi-modal fusion algorithm selection and tuning may require domain expertise not traditionally in 'Safety Services'. Mitigation: clarify that Safety Services owns orchestration and validation, not algorithm R&D; algorithms can be pluggable.

Risk: Vehicle subsystem must still own raw sensor drivers and hardware-level calibration. Ensure clear boundary: Vehicle provides raw Telemetry; Safety Services consumes and fuses. Mitigation: document sensor driver interface contract explicitly.

Risk: Teleoperation may need low-latency access to spatial entities for remote visualization. Mitigation: design SafetyAlert interface with streaming capability and consider caching validated entities at Teleoperation boundary.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is directly verifiable against the IM boundary (Safety Services) through: (1) schema validation outputs (machine-readable spatial entities conforming to declared geometry primitives and safety attributes), (2) acceptance criteria alignment ('worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions'), and (3) interface observability (SafetyAlert emits validated entities; Telemetry ingests raw observations). Traceability chain is clear: raw sensor data → fusion orchestration → schema-validated spatial model → downstream consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. Safety Services ownership is unambiguous and well-justified. The IM boundary explicitly owns: (a) declarative feature schema definition, (b) schema validation enforcement, (c) safety attribute mapping, (d) multi-modal fusion orchestration. Complementary responsibilities are clearly delegated: Vehicle owns raw sensor drivers and hardware; Teleoperation and NGHE Solution own consumption/planning logic. The decoupling principle (schema/what vs. sensor modality/how) is architecturally preserved at the IM level.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 + DP1 (declarative schema + multi-modal fusion) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. The DP logically solves the problem: (1) declarative schema provides the machine-readable model structure (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints), (2) multi-modal fusion orchestration provides the acquisition and mapping mechanism to populate that model from heterogeneous sensors. The decoupling of schema from sensor modality is a sound design principle that enables independent evolution. Acceptance criteria ('worksite model/map generated and available') are satisfied by the DP's output (validated spatial entities). No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Moderate: Multi-modal fusion algorithm selection and tuning expertise may not traditionally reside in Safety Services. Mitigation: clarify that Safety Services owns orchestration and validation logic (pluggable algorithm framework), not algorithm R&D. Algorithms can be developed/tuned externally and integrated as pluggable components.

Moderate: Fusion latency and real-time performance constraints must be explicitly managed. If fusion pipeline becomes a bottleneck, pressure may arise to push logic into Vehicle or edge processors, fragmenting the schema validation responsibility. Mitigation: establish latency budgets early; design pipeline with explicit performance gates; consider edge validation for time-critical modalities while maintaining schema conformance at Safety Services.

Low-Moderate: Schema evolution (Tasking interface) introduces versioning complexity. Downstream consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) may diverge in schema interpretation if versioning is not strictly enforced. Mitigation: implement schema versioning and deprecation policies; document breaking changes; consider schema registry pattern.

Low: Vehicle subsystem boundary must remain crisp. Vehicle owns raw sensor drivers and hardware calibration; Safety Services consumes Telemetry (post-driver, pre-fusion). Risk of creep: Safety Services may be pressured to own sensor-specific calibration logic. Mitigation: document sensor driver interface contract explicitly; establish clear handoff point (raw observations → Telemetry interface).

Low: Teleoperation may require low-latency streaming access to spatial entities for remote visualization. Current SafetyAlert interface design should support streaming/subscription patterns. Mitigation: design SafetyAlert with event streaming capability; consider caching validated entities at Teleoperation boundary to reduce round-trip latency.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Containment score of 0.92 for Safety Services is well-justified. The IM boundary is cohesive: schema definition, validation, and fusion orchestration are tightly coupled safety-critical functions that belong together. The decoupling principle is preserved: Safety Services does not own sensor hardware (Vehicle does) or planning logic (NGHE Solution does), only the schema and fusion orchestration. External interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking in, Network Link inout) are well-defined and support the cross-cutting nature of the module. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and unavoidable: Vehicle must own drivers; Teleoperation may need read-only access. No evidence of scattered responsibilities or hidden dependencies. Recommended action: proceed with Safety Services as primary owner; establish clear interface contracts and latency budgets to mitigate identified risks.

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit latency budgets for the fusion pipeline (e.g., max end-to-end latency from raw sensor observation to validated spatial entity emission). Document performance gates and consider edge processing for time-critical modalities (e.g., LiDAR obstacle detection) while maintaining schema conformance at Safety Services.

Define schema versioning and deprecation policy. Specify how schema updates (via Tasking interface) are communicated to downstream consumers and how backward compatibility is maintained. Consider schema registry or version negotiation pattern.

Document the sensor driver interface contract between Vehicle and Safety Services. Specify: (a) raw observation format (Telemetry), (b) sensor metadata (calibration, modality, timestamp), (c) error/uncertainty representation. Establish clear handoff point to prevent creep of sensor-specific logic into Safety Services.

Design SafetyAlert interface with streaming/subscription capability to support low-latency remote visualization in Teleoperation scenarios. Consider event-driven architecture (e.g., publish-subscribe) rather than request-response to reduce latency.

Clarify that Safety Services owns fusion orchestration and validation logic (pluggable framework), not algorithm R&D. Establish mechanism for integrating externally-developed fusion algorithms (e.g., plugin architecture, algorithm registry) to avoid knowledge silos.

Add acceptance criterion: 'Validated spatial entities conform to declared schema and are available to planning/execution functions within [X] ms latency budget.' This makes the FR-IM mapping verifiable and testable.

Document read-only access pattern for Teleoperation and NGHE Solution to validated spatial entities. Specify whether these consumers can subscribe to entity updates or must poll SafetyAlert interface.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR4 is well-formed, logically sound (FR-DP), and architecturally feasible (DP-IM) with high verification potential. However, the identified diffusion risks—particularly fusion latency, schema versioning, and algorithm integration—are non-trivial and require decomposition into explicit sub-FRs to ensure they are not deferred as implementation details. Safety criticality (high) demands that latency budgets, schema governance, and algorithm pluggability be elevated to functional requirements rather than treated as post-hoc constraints. Proceeding to next-level decomposition will make these concerns traceable, verifiable, and owned.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Orchestrate multi-modal sensor fusion with bounded latency to ensure real-time spatial model updates meet safety-critical timing constraints.
FR4.2: Enforce schema versioning and backward compatibility to enable safe evolution of spatial feature definitions without breaking downstream consumers.
FR4.3: Support pluggable fusion algorithm integration to decouple algorithm R&D from Safety Services core orchestration logic.
FR4.4: Validate sensor observations against declared metadata (calibration, modality, uncertainty) to ensure fusion input quality and traceability.
FR4.5: Provide streaming/subscription access to validated spatial entities for low-latency remote visualization and planning system consumption.

revise.why: FR4 acceptance criteria should be augmented to explicitly include latency budget, schema versioning governance, and algorithm pluggability as functional requirements rather than design constraints. Current FR ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is correct but under-specifies the safety-critical operational constraints that the gate analysis identified. Revised FR4 should read: 'Capture, validate, and represent worksite spatial features in a versioned, machine-readable model with bounded latency and pluggable fusion algorithms, ensuring schema conformance and traceability across heterogeneous sensors.' This elevation prevents diffusion risks from becoming hidden technical debt.

assumptions: Safety Services will own fusion orchestration and validation logic (pluggable framework), not algorithm R&D itself.
Latency budget for end-to-end fusion (raw observation → validated entity emission) is feasible within safety-critical timing constraints (to be quantified in FR4.1 decomposition).
Schema versioning can be managed via registry pattern or similar governance mechanism without introducing unacceptable complexity.
Downstream consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) can tolerate schema versioning and will implement version negotiation or subscription patterns.
Edge processing for time-critical modalities (e.g., LiDAR) is architecturally acceptable as long as schema validation remains centralized at Safety Services.
open_risks: Fusion latency may become a bottleneck if multi-modal orchestration is not carefully designed. Risk of pressure to fragment validation logic into Vehicle or edge processors, violating containment principle. Mitigation: FR4.1 decomposition must establish explicit latency budgets and performance gates.

Schema versioning complexity may grow if downstream consumers diverge in interpretation. Risk of silent failures or incompatibility. Mitigation: FR4.2 decomposition must define strict versioning policy and breaking-change communication protocol.

Algorithm pluggability may introduce security or safety gaps if external algorithms are not properly vetted. Risk of unsafe fusion outputs. Mitigation: FR4.3 decomposition must define algorithm validation and certification criteria.

Sensor metadata quality (calibration, uncertainty) may be inconsistent across Vehicle drivers. Risk of garbage-in-garbage-out in fusion pipeline. Mitigation: FR4.4 decomposition must define metadata schema and validation rules.

Streaming/subscription access to spatial entities may introduce new latency or consistency challenges for remote Teleoperation. Risk of stale or inconsistent entity views. Mitigation: FR4.5 decomposition must define consistency model and caching strategy.
evidence_needed: Latency budget quantification: specify max end-to-end latency from raw sensor observation to validated spatial entity emission, broken down by modality and fusion stage.
Schema versioning policy document: define versioning scheme, deprecation timeline, breaking-change communication, and backward-compatibility guarantees.
Algorithm integration framework design: specify pluggable algorithm interface, validation criteria, certification process, and integration testing requirements.
Sensor metadata schema: define required metadata fields (calibration, modality, timestamp, uncertainty), validation rules, and error handling.
Streaming/subscription interface design: specify event schema, subscription patterns, consistency guarantees, and caching strategy for Teleoperation and NGHE Solution consumers.
Performance testing results: demonstrate that multi-modal fusion pipeline meets latency budgets under realistic sensor load and network conditions.
Interface contract documentation: formalize Telemetry (Vehicle → Safety Services), SafetyAlert (Safety Services → consumers), and Tasking (consumers → Safety Services) interface specifications.
